segments.s475-g97: ازین پس is the compound 'از این پس' (poetic orthography) meaning 'after this' or 'from now on'. بیاید is a classical/poetic present/subjunctive form meaning 'will come' or 'may come' (it announces an approaching time). یکی روزگار is an indefinite noun phrase 'a certain time' (یکی here = 'a certain / one') that serves as the subject of بیاید.
segments.s475-g98: که introduces a subordinate (relative/temporal) clause 'when/that'. درویش is the subject 'the dervish' and گردد (classical present/subjunctive of شدن/گشتن) means 'becomes'. چنان modifies the following adjectives ('so' / 'such'), and سست و خوار are coordinated adjectival predicates 'weak and despised'.
segments.s475-g99: که‌گر is an archaic/poetic conditional 'if'. ابر is the subject 'cloud(s)', گردد again is 'becomes', and بهاران here indicates time 'in spring' (or 'the spring'), while پرآب is an adjective 'full of water / well‑watered'. The clause sets a conditional circumstance.
segments.s475-g100: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. درویش is the indirect object 'from the dervish' (i.e., 'from the dervish's sight/benefit'). پنهان کند is a verb phrase where پنهان (here used as a verbal root 'to hide') + کند ('does/makes') yields 'hides' or 'conceals'. آفتاب at the end is the subject 'the sun' placed after the verb by poetic inversion: literally 'from the dervish hides the sun.'
segments.s475-g101: نبارد is the negative present of باریدن 'it does not rain / does not fall (as rain)'. بدو is the enclitic/prepositional 'to him / upon him'. نیز 'also / even' emphasizes that not even his own (خویش) rain (باران) falls to him. باران خویش is a possessive phrase 'his own rain' formed from باران + خویش (here split as خوی + ش in the orthography).
segments.s475-g102: This clause is terse/poetic: دلِ مردِ درویش is a genitive chain 'the heart of the dervish man' (i.e. the dervish's heart). زو 'زو' = 'from it / because of that' connects to the previous clause. گشته is the perfect/participial sense 'has become / turned into' and ریش is the predicative noun 'a beard' — literally 'his heart has become a beard', a poetic/metaphorical expression; note the subject–predicate order and the enclitic conjunction و appears in the line for metrical/orthographic reasons.
segments.s475-g103: توانگر is the subject 'the wealthy / rich man'. ببخشد is the classical present/subjunctive form of بخشیدن used here in the sense 'gives / bestows'. همی is a frequentative/classical adverb 'always / continually'. این is the demonstrative object 'this', and بران (poetic form of 'برای آن' or 'بر آن') indicates direction 'upon that / to that one' — together: 'the rich man always bestows this on that one.'
segments.s475-g104: یکی here = 'one (person)' and با دگر = 'and another / with another' (ellipsis: the verb 'gives' from the previous segment is understood for both). چرب is an adjective literally 'oily' used figuratively 'flattering', and شیرین‌زبان is a compound adjective 'sweet‑tongued / glib' — together describing the second person who receives favors: 'another (who is) flattering and sweet‑tongued.'
segments.s475-g137: ز is the archaic/poetic short form of از meaning 'from.' نزدیک دانا functions as a prepositional phrase 'from near the sage' (نزدیک before the noun for poetic ordering). چو introduces a temporal clause 'when,' and برگشت is the past tense verb 'returned' that here precedes its subject شاه (poetic inversion), so چو برگشت شاه = 'when the king returned.'
segments.s475-g138: حکیمان is the plural subject 'the sages' (an older plural of حکیم). برفتند is the 3rd person plural past 'they went/departed.' با او is the prepositional phrase 'with him' (referring to the king), and براه is an archaic fused form meaning 'on the road' or 'along the way' (literally 'to/onto the road'). Together: 'the sages went with him on the road.'
segments.s475-g65: به کردار ماهی به دریا شود is built from two prepositional phrases plus the verb شود. به کردار ماهی ('in the manner/conduct of a fish') uses به as an adverbial/semblative marker and کردار as 'conduct/behavior'. به دریا شود literally 'becomes to the sea' — classical Persian uses شدن with a place to mean 'come/go to' or 'end up in'. The verb شود is third‑person singular (present/habitual/epic tense) meaning 'becomes/ends up'.
segments.s475-g66: گر introduces a conditional ('if/even if'). از here has a causal/ablative force ('because of / by reason of'), taking بدکنش as the source/agent of the action ('his bad conduct' or 'ill‑behaving'). بر ثریا شود uses بر + proper noun to indicate movement/raising 'toward/to the Pleiades'; شود again is third‑person singular 'become / be placed'. The clause means 'even if by his bad deeds he should be set/raised to the Pleiades.'
segments.s475-g67: همی is an adverb marking continuous or habitual action ('continually/always'). تشنگان را is the definite object ('the thirsty') with را. بخواند is third‑person singular present/habitual of خواندن here meaning 'call/summon' (classical option 'bexānad' used in the sense 'he calls'). برآب is a prepositional/locative phrase 'to/at the water' (here literal: calling people to water).
segments.s475-g68: کس here is negative universal 'no one' (common in poetry). او را marks the person (him) as indirect object/topic. ز is the poetic form of 'from' meaning 'by/from (learning)' and links to دانش 'knowledge/learning'. نسازد is ن + سازد (subjunctive/present form of ساختن) meaning 'does not/cannot make/compose'; joined with جواب it means 'compose an answer' — the whole clause: 'no one can compose for him an answer from learning.'
segments.s475-g69: گریزند is third‑person plural present/habitual 'they flee/escape'. زان is the archaic poetic preposition 'from that' (زان = از + آن). مرد is the noun 'man' and دانش‌پژوه is a compound noun 'scholar/learned man' modifying مرد. The phrase means 'they flee from that scholar.'
segments.s475-g70: گشایند is third‑person plural present/habitual of گشودن 'they open' (classical form). لبها is the object 'the lips.' به here introduces the relation 'toward/among' and بد هم‌گروه is a compound meaning 'bad companions / of bad company' (هم‌گروه 'of the same group' used as 'companions'). Together: 'they open their lips in the company of the wicked.'
segments.s475-g71: به here functions as a topicalizer 'as for / concerning the fifth.' که دیدی is a relative clause 'which you saw' (که = 'that/which'; دیدی = 'you saw'). یکی شارستان places 'one town' (یکی 'one / a certain') as the predicated item — literal order is topical particle + relative + numeral + noun, typical of Ferdowsi's concise style.
segments.s475-g72: بدو is the enclitic prepositional 'to it / in it' (here 'within it'). اندرون means 'inside/within.' ساخته is the passive/perfect participle of ساختن 'made/constructed' and carries a perfective sense 'has built.' کارستان is a nominal, here 'a feat / marvelous work' describing what was built. The clause means 'within it he has built a marvel.'
segments.s475-g41: Negation with نه precedes the nominal phrase; word order is Subject (کرپاس نغز) + oblique phrase (از کشیدن) + verb (درید) at the end, a common classical Persian SOV order. از + verbal noun کشیدن expresses cause/means (“from/through pulling”), and درید is a past-tense verb (it tore / was torn); the whole means “did not tear from being pulled.”
segments.s475-g42: The second negated clause mirrors the first (نه ... نه ...). آمد ستوه is an idiomatic sequence meaning “became weary” (آمد here = ‘came/turned (into)’ + predicate ستوه ‘weary’). آنک introduces a relative clause “he who…,” and او را کشید puts the agent/object before the main verb کشید (past of کشیدن, ‘to pull’); را marks او as the definite object of کشید. Note: the provided senses list lacks the pulling sense for کشید, so that sense is marked -1.
segments.s475-g43: ازین پس is a temporal phrase meaning “after this / henceforth” (از + این written as ازین + پس). بیاید is subjunctive/optative here functioning as future/expected arrival “will come” or “may come,” and یکی نامدار is an indefinite nominal “a certain/one renowned (man).”
segments.s475-g44: ز (poetic variant of از) marks origin “from,” followed by the place/people phrase دشت سواران “the plain’s horsemen.” نیزه گزار is a compound: نیزه (spear) + گزار (agent-form), meaning “spear-bearer/spearman”; the provided senses for گزار do not reflect this agentive meaning and so are marked -1. The whole segment describes his provenance and role (“a spearman from among the plain horsemen”).
segments.s475-g45: یکی مرد is an introductory indefinite “a certain man.” Adjectives follow the noun in classical Persian here: مرد پاکیزه و نیکخوی = “a man clean/pure and of good disposition.” The conjunction و simply links the two adjectives.
segments.s475-g46: بدو is the dative “to him.” دین یزدان is the object phrase “the religion of God,” and شود is the present/past-future auxiliary meaning “become / be established.” چارسوی (“four‑sides”) here means “in all directions / everywhere,” placed at the end as an adverbial. The clause therefore says that God’s religion will come to / be established for him everywhere.
segments.s475-g47: یکی introduces an indefinite person “a certain.” پیر here means “old” (adjective before/after noun varies in poetry), followed by دهقان “peasant/farmer” and the compound آتش‌پرست “fire‑worshipper” describing his belief; the three together name the person (“an old peasant, a fire‑worshipper”).
segments.s475-g48: که introduces a relative clause “who/that….” بر + واژ gives a sense of reversal/overturning (واژ = overturned); برسم is from رسیدن (‘to reach/attain’ or ‘to befall’) used here in a construction with بگیرد (present-subjunctive/optative of گرفتن) meaning “will take/seize.” بگیرد بدست is an idiom “take in hand / seize in the hand.” Altogether the clause means “who will take in hand to overturn (the custom).” (Because the provided senses do not exactly match the poetic compounds, some lexical senses are approximated below.)
segments.s475-g81: ستاینده is an active present-participial noun 'the one(s) who praise' functioning as the subject (here plural). مرد نادان is a noun + adjective phrase ('man/person foolish') with the adjective after the noun (typical Persian order). شوند is the 3rd-person plural present form of شدن 'become' (literary/poetic - شوند rather than modern می‌شوند), so the clause reads 'those who praise become foolish/ignorant men.'
segments.s475-g82: نیایش کنان is a converb/adverbial participle ('praying', literally 'doing prayer') that gives the manner or attendant action. پیش یزدان is a prepositional phrase 'before/in the presence of God' (پیش = 'in front of/in the presence of'). شوند again is 3rd-person plural of شدن 'become/come' used here with parallel structure to the previous clause: 'praying, they come before God.'
segments.s475-g83: همی داند is a prefixed/emphatic habitual present 'one always/ever knows' (همی is a literary adverb of continuance/emphasis placed before the verb). آنکس که گوید دروغ is a relative clause: آنکس 'that person' + که 'who' + گوید (3rd sg. present of گفتن here 'says/tells') + دروغ 'a lie' — 'one always recognizes the person who utters a lie.'
segments.s475-g84: همی here is emphatic ('indeed/so/therefore') introducing the consequence. زان + پرستش = 'from that worship' (زان = 'from that/therefore'), with پرستش the object. نگیرد is negative present/subjunctive 'does not take/receive' (ن‑ + گیرد), and فروغ 'radiance/glory' is the thing not received, placed at the end for emphasis. The whole clause: 'therefore from that worship he does not obtain radiance.'
segments.s475-g85: ششم is the ordinal/adverbial 'sixth' (introducing the next item). آنک دیدی is 'that which you saw' (آنک = 'that which/that who', دیدی = 2nd-person past 'you saw'). بر اسپی دو سر is a locative/prepositional phrase 'upon/on a two‑headed steed' (بر = 'on', اسپی = 'steed/horse', دو سر = 'two‑headed' where the numeral + noun follows the noun it modifies).
segments.s475-g86: خورش را is the direct object with the object marker را (خورش here 'food/dish' in the lexical entry given). نبودی is an older/past form of بودن with negative prefix (here 'was not' or 'did not exist/occur'); in poetry forms like نبودی can be used where modern Persian would use نبود/نیست. بروبر is an adverb 'face-to-face/straight across' modifying گذر 'passage/pass'. The clause literal order is object (خورش را) + verb (نبودی) + adverbial phrase (بروبر گذر), giving 'the food did not pass directly/face‑to‑face.'
segments.s475-g87: زمانی بیاید is a common prophetic formula 'a time will come' (زمانی = 'a time/moment', بیاید = 3rd-singular present/subjunctive 'will come' in literary usage). که introduces the subordinate clause 'that/when', and مردم به چیز marks the subject and its oblique complement ('people' + 'with/at a thing' — به چیز will link as the object of becoming in the next segment).
segments.s475-g88: شود is the 3rd-person singular of شدن 'become' (here governing the state of مردم introduced earlier). شاد 'happy/pleased' is the predicate, and the conjunction و links the next clause. سیری نیابند is 'they do not find/attain satiety' — نیابند = ن‑ + یابند (3rd‑pl. present/subjunctive 'do not find/obtain'). نیز at the end is an emphatic 'also/likewise.' Overall: 'people will become pleased with a thing and, likewise, will not find satiety.'
segments.s475-g73: پر از is a fixed adjective phrase meaning “full of X”; the preposition از follows پر to mark the thing it is full of. The sequence خورد و داد و خرید و فروخت lists kinds of exchange/consumption (literally “eating and giving and purchase and selling”) in a poetic, piled-up coordination (و = “and”) describing bustling commerce or transactions.
segments.s475-g74: تو گفتی here is a parenthetical / colloquial comment — literally “you said” or rhetorically “one might say.” زمان is the temporal adverb “at that time.” چشم ایشان بدوخت is a simple past/passive-like construction: چشم (subject) + ایشان (possessive/agent marker “their”) + بدوخت (“was sewn” / “sewed up”), i.e. “their eyes were sewn (shut).”
segments.s475-g75: ز کوری is a prepositional/causal phrase “from/because of blindness” (ز = “from/because of”, کوری = “blindness”). یکی دیگری را ندید is Subject–Object–Verb order with را marking the definite object: “one (یکی) did not see (ندید) the other (دیگری را).” ندید is negated past (ن‑ + دید).
segments.s475-g76: This line uses elliptical, archaic demonstrative phrasing. همی here is a linking adverb (“thus/so” or “indeed”); the pairs این بدان / آن بدین are shorthand for “this toward that / that toward this” where the short preposition ب (be/“to/toward”) prefixes the demonstratives (بدان = به آن, بدین = به این). ننگرید is the negated past-form of نگرِیدن (ن + گری / نگری + د) meaning “did not look / did not regard.” The whole clause therefore says they paid no heed to one another.
segments.s475-g77: زمانی بیاید is a future-temporal clause: زمانی = “a time / someday,” بیاید = jussive/subjunctive/poetic future “will come.” کزان سان شود is a relative/result clause: کزان = “from which,” سان = “such/like,” شود = “become / happen,” i.e. “a time will come from which things will be such.”
segments.s475-g78: که introduces the content clause “that ….” دانا is the adjective/noun “the wise.” پرستار here is used as “adorer/worshipper” or figuratively “one who flatters/serves.” نادان is “the ignorant.” شود is the verbal auxiliary “becomes.” Word order is Subject (دانا) + Predicate (پرستار نادان شود): “that the wise will become the worshipper/servant of the ignorant.”
segments.s475-g79: بدیشان بود is the inverted dative-subject construction: بدیشان = به ایشان (“to/for them”), بود = “was.” دانشومند (scholar, learned man) is the noun subject, and خوار is the predicate/adjective “despised/held cheap.” Literally: “A learned man was despised by them.”
segments.s475-g80: درخت خردشان is a metaphorical nominal phrase: درخت = “tree,” خردشان = “their wisdom” (خرد + possessive -شان). نیاید is negative present/future “does not come / does not appear / does not bear”; به بار means “to fruit / into load” (بار = yield/fruit). Word order is Subject (درخت خردشان) + Verb (نیاید) + adjunct (به بار): “the tree of their wisdom does not bear fruit.”
segments.s475-g57: This chunk contains topical items before the verb: تو functions as a discourse particle (here closer to 'then' or a deictic) rather than the usual direct address; کرپاس را is the definite object (را marks the definite direct object: “the shroud/coarse cloth”); دین یزدان‌شناس is a compound attributive phrase ('religion‑ and God‑knowing' / 'pious, God‑knowing') that modifies the people who will be named by the following noun. Word order is classical/poetic: modifiers and objects can precede the verb.
segments.s475-g58: کشنده چهار is the nominal phrase 'four killers/slayers' (the numeral چهار follows in literary word order to indicate 'the four'); آمد is the past/present narrative verb 'came' (3rd person); از بهر پاس is a prepositional phrase 'for the sake of the guard/for guarding' (از بهر = 'for/on account of', پاس = 'guard/sentry'). Together they form the verbal clause 'four slayers came for the guard.'
segments.s475-g59: همی is an iterative/adverbial particle meaning 'continually/always' and precedes the verb to show repeated action. درکشد is a classical present/continuous form from کشیدن with the prefix در- meaning 'pulls/draws' (here 'draws away' or 'takes'); the sequence این ازان آن ازین lists contrasting demonstratives (this from that, that from this) in inverted/poetic order — short pronoun phrases serving as the objects of درکشد. The final 'ین' is the orthographic/poetic form of 'این'.
segments.s475-g60: شوند is the 3rd person plural present/subjunctive of شدن 'become/occur' (here 'will be/ arise'); آن زمان is a temporal adverbial 'at that time' (classical placement after the verb is normal in poetry); دشمن is the predicate noun 'enemies'; از بهر دین is a prepositional phrase 'on account of/for religion' (از بهر = 'for the sake of', دین = 'religion'). The whole clause means 'at that time enemies will arise because of religion.'
segments.s475-g61: دگر is 'another' or 'one more'; تشنه‌ای is an indefinite nominal 'a thirsty one' (تشنه + enclitic -ای); کو is an archaic relative/interrogative particle here functioning like 'who'/'which' (classical usage); شد is past 'became'; از آب خوش is a prepositional phrase 'from/away from the sweet/pleasant water' (از + آب خوش). Together: 'another, a thirsty one who became estranged from sweet water.'
segments.s475-g62: گریزان is a participial/adjectival form 'fleeing / avoiding' describing the person; و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'; ماهی is literal 'fish' used here as a simile or image; ورا is an adverb/pronoun meaning 'to it / thither' (pointing toward something previously mentioned); آب‌کش is a compound noun 'colander/sieve for water' — imageically, the fish is bound for the sieve (i.e., caught). The line uses compressed poetic imagery: the thirsty man who shuns good water is likened to a fish headed into a sieve.
segments.s475-g63: زمانی بیاید که is a common classical phrasing 'a time will come that...' (زمانی = 'a time', بیاید = 'may come / will come', که introduces the subordinate clause). پاکیزه مرد is the noun phrase 'a pure/clean man' (پاکیزه = 'pure/clean', مرد = 'man/person'); it is the subject of the following verb.
segments.s475-g64: شود is the 3rd person singular of شدن 'become' (here future/resultive 'will become'); خوار is the predicate adjective 'despised/ruined'; چون is used in the sense 'as/when' (classical, introducing the circumstance); آب دانش is a genitive compound 'the water of knowledge' (metaphor for learning); بخورد is the 3rd person singular present/subjunctive of خوردن 'to eat/drink' — here 'to take' or 'to drink' the water of knowledge. Grammatically the clause states the result: 'he will be despised as/when he drinks the water of knowledge.'
segments.s475-g25: چو is a conditional/subordinating particle meaning “if” here, introducing the protasis. بر کار تو رای فرخ کنیم is literally “we make/apply the counsel (رای) of/for your matter”: بر + کار (“regarding the matter”) places رای (counsel/plan) with the noun it qualifies, تو is the possessor/target “your,” and کنیم is first-person-plural present/subjunctive (we do/let us do/if we do) used in the conditional clause. فرخ here functions as an adjective/name attached to رای (see senses).
segments.s475-g26: همان خواب را نیز پاسخ کنیم is the apodosis (main/result clause) with کنیم again marking the first-person-plural action “we will/do (respond).” همان خواب را is the definite object (را marks it) “that same dream,” نیز adds “also/likewise,” and پاسخ is the noun “answer/response” used with کنيم to mean “answer/respond to.” The verb form mirrors the conditional clause, giving a parallel structure.
segments.s475-g27: یکی خانه دیدی uses یکی as an indefinite/article-like “a certain/one” before خانه (“house”), and دیدی is the simple past second-person singular “you saw.” The phrase is straightforward SOV in classical/poetic order: (one) (house) (you saw).
segments.s475-g28: کزو پیل بیرون شدی بی‌درنگ is a clause starting with کزو (“from which / from whom”), linking back to سوراخ in the previous segment. پیل بیرون شدی is an intransitive event “an elephant went/issued out”: بیرون is an adverbial (“out/away”), شدی is the past/aspectual verb indicating the occurrence (poetic/archaic past form), and بی‌درنگ is an adverb “immediately.”
segments.s475-g29: تو آن خانه را همچو گیتی شناس places the topic/object آن خانه را (“that house” with را) after the pronoun تو (“you”), and همچو گیتی شناس is a simile/description “like a world-knower” modifying تو (you) — i.e., ‘you, as one who knows the world.’ Word order is emphatic/poetic: pronoun + object + descriptive phrase.
segments.s475-g30: همان پیل شاهی بود ناسپاس is a nominal clause with بود as the copula in the past (“was”); همان پیل شاهی is the subject phrase “that same royal elephant,” and ناسپاس is a postpositive adjective/predicate “ungrateful.” The order (subject + بود + adjective) is typical of older/poetic Persian.
segments.s475-g31: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause “who/that,” here giving qualities of the referenced person/thing. بیدادگر باشد is a present/subjunctive or characteristic clause “he is/cruel (a tyrant)” with باشد marking existence/being in a formal/poetic register. و connects to کژ گوی — a compound where کژ (“crooked/wrong/perverse”) modifies گوی (from گفتن/گوی — “speaker/saying”), yielding “perverse speaker/one who speaks wrongly.”
segments.s475-g32: جز از نام شاهی نباشد بدوی is a negated restrictive clause: جز از نام شاهی (“except/from the name/title of kingship”) is the only thing admitted, نباشد is a subjunctive/negative copula meaning “there is not / there be not,” and بدوی (here adjectival) means “original” or “substantial.” Together the clause says there is nothing original in him except the mere name of kingship — i.e., name without substance.
segments.s475-g17: چهارم is the ordinal 'fourth' modifying the following noun; قدح is the head noun 'goblet/cup'. کاندرو ریزی is a compound modifier placed before آب (an adjectival or attributive phrase built from کاندرو + ریزی) and describes the kind/quality of the water; آب is the noun 'water' that completes the noun phrase. The order (ordinal → noun → modifier → substance) is a typical poetic compression.
segments.s475-g18: نه ... نه gives a 'neither ... nor ...' negation. ز before آتش marks cause/agency ('by/from fire'), شود is the present/become verb ('become/is made'), and کم here functions as a predicate meaning 'less/insufficient' (i.e. 'become less'). از introduces the second agent ('by/from the sun'), آفتاب. Grammatically the clause says the water 'does not become less' by either agent.
segments.s475-g19: ز خوردن is a causal expression 'from/by eating' (ز + infinitive/masdar), نگیرد is a present negative-form verb meaning 'does not take/lose' (here 'is not taken away'), کمی modifies آب ('a little of the water'), and اوی is a possessive/pronominal element 'its/his/its own' attached to آب. Together: 'it does not lose a little of its water by eating' (i.e. 'eating will not take much of its water').
segments.s475-g20: بدین چیزها is a prepositional phrase 'with/by these things' (ب = 'by/to'; دین here is part of the fused demonstrative 'بدین' — the provided lemma 'دین' does not fit semantically and is marked below). راست کن is an imperative: راست = 'straight/right/correct' and کن = 'do' → 'set/make right'; آب روی is a noun + postposition where روی = 'on/upon', so 'place/pour the water on (its) surface'. The clause is an instruction: 'with these things, make the water lie on the surface.'
segments.s475-g21: چو آید begins a temporal clause ('when it comes' — چو = 'when', آید = 'comes'). بدین باش is an imperative/advisory 'be/act by this' (بدین = 'by/according to this', باش = 'be' or 'follow'); و ties the next noun phrase مسگال جنگ, where مسگال is a small unit/measure and جنگ is the measured noun. Grammatically the line mixes an imperative with a noun phrase that supplies an additional item or quantity to follow the instruction.
segments.s475-g22: چو خواهی که introduces a conditional/wish ('if/when you wish that...'); که is the complementizer 'that'. ایدر is the locative 'here'. نسازد is a negative present/subjunctive verb 'does not/may not make' (from ساختن 'to make/compose'); درنگ is the noun 'delay/pausing'. The clause literally: 'if you want that here he not make a delay' → 'if you wish that there be no delay here.'
segments.s475-g23: بسنده is an adjective/adverb 'sufficient/enough'. نباشی here functions as a present/subjunctive/negative copula meaning 'you are not' or 'you will not be'; the personal pronoun تو is explicit 'you'. با لشکرش is a prepositional phrase 'with/against his army' (با + لشکر + possessive -ش). Together: 'you will not be sufficient with/against his army.'
segments.s475-g24: This is a coordinated negation introduced by نه ... و ... و ...: نه با چاره و گنج و با افسرش lists the means that will not suffice. با introduces each means ('with/by'); چاره = 'expedient/strategy', گنج = 'treasure', and افسرش here is best read as 'his officer/commander' (not 'helmet'). The whole line: 'not by stratagem, nor by treasure, nor by his commander.'
segments.s475-g89: نه before the subject creates a 'neither' negative clause. درویش is the subject, یابد is the present/future third-person singular of یابیدن ('will obtain/find'), and ازو is the archaic fused prepositional phrase (از + او) meaning 'from it/him'. بهره‌ای is an indefinite object 'a share/benefit' (the -ی suffix makes it 'a/an'). The verb applies to the parallel items that follow.
segments.s475-g90: The repeated نه ... و نه ... construction continues the negative coordination ('nor ... nor ...'). دانش‌پژوهی is an abstract noun 'scholarship/learning' and شهره‌ای is 'renown/fame' with the -ای suffix giving an indefinite/partitive sense ('any fame'). The verb from the previous segment (یابد) is understood to govern these items as well.
segments.s475-g91: جز introduces an exception ('except'). از خویشتن ('from themselves') is the source phrase; را marks the phrase as a topical/object phrase. نخواهند is the negated third-person plural present/future of خواستن ('they will not want/seek' or idiomatically 'they will have no recourse'), and بس here means 'enough'/'only' — together the clause conveys 'they will have no one but themselves' or 'they will depend only on themselves.'
segments.s475-g92: کسی را with را marks the (non‑existent) person as the object/topic. نباشند is the negative third‑person plural of بودن used impersonally here ('there will not be'), and فریادرس is a predicate noun 'rescuer/one who answers the cry for help.' Together: 'There will be no one to come to their aid.'
segments.s475-g93: به هفتم is an adverbial/time phrase 'on the seventh' (به + ordinal). که introduces the subordinate clause ('when/that'), پرآب is a compound adjective 'full of water/well-watered', دیدی is the second-person singular past 'you saw', and سه خم is the numeral + noun 'three jars/vessels' (خم here = earthen jar). The clause addresses or narrates to the addressee in the second person.
segments.s475-g94: یکی زو ('one of them') uses ز (poetic for از) + او (here written separately) to mean 'of/from it/them.' تهی مانده is a participial phrase 'remained empty' (تهی = empty, مانده = remaining/left), forming a predicative clause. بد is an evaluative adjective 'bad,' and تا بدم is a purposive/extentive linkage where بدم ('my blood' figuratively = my harm) expresses that the situation is to the speaker's hurt; together the final phrase means 'bad to my harm' or 'harmful to me.'
segments.s475-g95: The clause begins with the numeral دو 'two' as the subject. از آب دایم indicates source/contents 'of/permanent water' (i.e., filled with perennial water), سراسر is an adverb 'throughout/entirely,' and بدی here functions as a predicate adjective/noun 'bad/ill' with the copula omitted (understood 'were bad' or 'were of bad consequence'). The copula 'were' is implied for the plural subject.
segments.s475-g96: میانه یکی 'the middle one' (میانه = middle; یکی = one) is the subject of the following description. خشک 'dry' and بی‌بر 'without offspring/fruitless' are coordinate adjectives joined by و. بدی again is a predicate 'bad/ill' with the copula omitted, describing the middle vessel as dry, barren, and unfortunate.
segments.s475-g105: شود here is an impersonal/habitual verb meaning “it happens / it becomes” that introduces the clause. مرد درویش را is a definite/emphatic nominal phrase referring to “the poor man,” with را marking the noun; خشک لب is a postpositive descriptive phrase “dry-lipped” (lit. “dry lip”) modifying that noun.
segments.s475-g106: همی gives a continuous/habitual sense (“continually / will”), روز را is the definite object marked by را, and بگذراند is the verb “to spend / pass (time).” به شب expresses direction/transition “into night.” Overall: “(he) spends the day into night.”
segments.s475-g107: دگر آنک = “the other, that (one),” where آنک functions as a demonstrative; گاوی names the animal (“a cow”). چنان تن درست is a postpositive adjectival phrase “of such a well-made body / so well-bodied,” with درست used adjectivally “proper / well-formed.”
segments.s475-g108: ز is the poetic preposition “from,” گوساله لاغر او = “her thin calf” (noun + adjective + third-person pronoun as possessor), and شیر جست is a verbal phrase “milk leapt/sprang (forth).” Word order is compact/poetic: source + possessor followed by the verb.
segments.s475-g109: چو is the temporal conjunction “when.” کیوان is the planet Saturn. به برج ترازو شود = “becomes/enters into the sign (برج) of the Scales (ترازو),” with شود = “becomes / comes to be” describing the astrological placement.
segments.s475-g110: جهان = “the world.” زیر نیروی بازو means “under the power/force of the arm,” where زیر = “under,” نیروی = “force/strength,” and بازو (literally “arm”) stands metaphorically for physical might; شود again marks the result “becomes / will be.”
segments.s475-g111: شود governs the coordinated noun phrases: کار بیمار = “work becomes ill/sick” and درویش سست = “the poor man (dرویش) becomes weak.” The verb (شود) is applied to both parts joined by و, yielding a parallel result.
segments.s475-g112: وزو is an archaic/poetic compound meaning roughly “from that / because of that” (here treated as a connective, not the modern noun meaning “ablution”), followed by چیز خواهد = “(he) will want/desire something.” همی gives an emphatic/continuous sense and تن‌درست is the thing desired “a sound/healthy body.” The clause thus means “and from that he will desire a healthy body.”
segments.s475-g113: نه هرگز (lit. “not ever/never”) negates the clause. گشاید is a classical present/epic third‑person verb meaning “opens/reveals” (not modern subjunctive here but Ferdowsian present). سر گنج literally “head/top/secret of the treasure” — سر is used idiomatically as the summit/secret and خویش is possession (his own) formed by خوی + the possessive ـش.
segments.s475-g114: نه introduces a second negative clause. ز (poetic از) = “from/of”; و here is part of the contracted sequence (ز + او → زو) and in the annotation stands alone, representing the object “it/him” (the treasure) — its sense is not given separately. باز دارد is a compound idea: باز here participates in the verbal compound (باز داشتن / باز داشتنِ چیزی) meaning “to remove/relieve/take away,” and دارد is the third‑person present of داشتن used with باز to form the verb “takes away / relieves.” به is the preposition linking the action to تن رنجِ خویش (“the suffering of his body”) — i.e. “by/for his body” or “to his body” in the sense “relieve the body of suffering.” خوی + ش again forms the possessive “his.”
segments.s475-g115: دگر = “another/other.” چشمه‌ای is the indefinite (–ی) of چشمه “a spring/source.” دیدی is second‑person past “you saw” (typical narrative address in Ferdowsi). از introduces the material/source relation “of/from,” followed by آب “water.” خشک literally means “dry,” but in poetic usage here it modifies the water’s character (contrastive — e.g. clear/unsullied or of the dry ground) and is a descriptive epithet rather than implying absence of water; it’s part of the poetic description of the spring.
segments.s475-g116: به گرد = a prepositional phrase “around/about.” اندرِش is اندر + ـش = “in/within it” (the genitive/locative reference to the spring). آبهای is the plural of آب “waters.” چو is the simile particle “like/as,” and مشک is “musk,” so this clause describes waters around the spring as being like musk (i.e. sweet/scented/precious).
segments.s475-g117: نه negates. ز (poetic از) = “from.” و again is the reduced form attached in the text (functioning as the pronoun object “it/that”) but is given separately in the annotation. بردمیدی is an archaic/past second‑person form meaning “you took/removed” (Ferdowsi’s older morphology). یکی here means “one/a single”; روشن qualifies آب as “bright/clear/transparent” (a descriptive epithet). The whole clause: “Nor did you take a single clear drop from it.”
segments.s475-g118: نه continues the negation into the next clause. آن آبها را: آن = “those/the,” آبها = “waters,” and را is the definite‑object marker. گرفتی is second‑person past “you seized/you took.” شتاب = “haste” and here functions adverbially (“in haste/with haste”). The clause says you did not seize those waters hastily (i.e. you did not hurry to take them).
segments.s475-g119: ازین پس = “after this” (از + این with the archaic spelling/segmentation); پس here means “after.” یکی روزگاری = “a certain time/once upon a time” (یکی is indefinite “a certain” and روزگاری is “time/age”). بود is simple past “there was.”
segments.s475-g120: که introduces the subordinate clause here with the meaning “when/that.” اندر = “in/within,” جهان = “the world,” شهریاری here denotes “sovereignty/royalty/kingly rule” (can imply a ruling king or realm), and بود is the past “was.” Together: “when there was kingship/sovereignty in the world.”
segments.s475-g1: چو + perfective بشنید here marks a temporal clause 'when he heard'. The verb بشنید (past) precedes the subject مهران, a common poetic verb–subject order. ز (poetic برابر of از) introduces the source/subject matter 'from the plot', and این سخن = 'this speech/word/news' is the object of ز.
segments.s475-g2: بدو گفت = 'he said to him'; بدو is the prepositional clitic 'to him' attached before گفت. ازین is the archaic fused preposition 'از + این' meaning 'from/with respect to this'. خواب is 'dream' and دل بد مکن is an imperative negation where مکن (do not) applies to the clause 'دل بد' = 'make (your) heart bad' → 'do not be troubled'.
segments.s475-g3: نه ... نه sets up a paired negative ('neither ... nor ...'). کمتر شود = 'become less' (شود is the present/subjunctive/habitual verb). بر تو indicates the beneficiary/affected person 'to you/for you'. نام بلند ('lofty name') is the predicate whose diminution is being denied.
segments.s475-g4: نه آید ... گزند = 'nor shall harm come'. آید is the present/subjunctive of آمدن 'to come/occur'. بدین is the archaic compound corresponding to به + این ('to/for this'), here marking 'this kingship' (پادشاهی). گزند is the object 'harm' that will not arrive.
segments.s475-g5: سکندر is the subject. بیارد is an archaic/poetic verbal form meaning 'will bring' (here the dataset's lemma senses do not list 'bring', so sense is marked -1). سپاهی گران is the object phrase 'a heavy/weighty (i.e. mighty) army', with the adjective after the noun as often in poetry.
segments.s475-g6: ز روم و ز ایران = 'from Rome and from Iran', the repeated ز is the poetic form of از. گزیده سران = 'chosen leaders' where گزیده 'selected' modifies سران 'leaders'; adjective follows noun in the poetic word order.
segments.s475-g7: چو خواهی که = 'if you want that...' where چو functions as a conditional 'if' and خواهی is 2nd-person present 'you wish'. باشد is the subjunctive/present 'may be / be' introducing the subordinate clause. ترا (split here as ت + را) marks the person concerned 'for you/your', and آب‌روی = 'reputation/good name' is the predicate whose continuance is being wished for.
segments.s475-g8: خرد یار کن is an imperative: کن 'make' with یار (ally/companion) as complement, so 'make wisdom your ally'. رزم او را مجوی is a negative imperative: مجوی 'do not seek' takes او را 'his (or that) battle' as object; word order places the object before the negated verb, typical in Classical Persian poetry.
segments.s475-g49: دگر دین موسی is a nominal phrase meaning “another (one), the religion of Moses.” دگر (preceding the noun) is an adjective/pronominal determiner “another.” که introduces a relative clause modifying دین موسی, and خوانی is a 2nd-person present form here meaning “you call” (classical/poetic -ی ending). جهود is the archaic word for “the Jews.” Word order is typical older Persian: determiner + noun + relative clause.
segments.s475-g50: که گوید begins the reported speech/relative clause “who say/that say.” گوید is the 3rd-person verb form “says” (the supplied sense list lacks the verb sense, so it is marked below as unknown). جز آن را نشاید ستود is a fixed construction: جز آن = “except that,” را is the object marker, نشاید is an impersonal/modal present meaning “it is not fitting/should not,” and ستود is the bare stem of ستودن used as an infinitive “to praise.” Together: “(they) say that nothing except that ought to be praised.”
segments.s475-g51: دگر دین یونانی آن پارسا: again 'دگر' marks another item; دین یونانی = “the Greek religion/faith” (یونانی modifies دین). آن پارسا is a demonstrative + adjective used substantively: “that pious one.” The copula is omitted (typical in poetry), so this whole chunk names the next faith/person.
segments.s475-g52: که introduces a relative clause: 'who brought justice into the king’s heart.' داد here is the noun 'justice' placed before آورد (a common Persian compound: noun + آورد = 'brought/caused'). آورد is past tense 'brought'; در دل پادشا is a prepositional phrase 'into the king’s heart' (در + noun phrase). Word order is object/prepositional phrase after the verb in this line, but the noun-before-verb compound is normal.
segments.s475-g53: چهارم is the ordinal 'the fourth' and is the sentence’s topic; بیاید is a present/subjunctive form often used as a future or optative 'will come' or 'may come.' همین پاک‌رای is the emphatic demonstrative + adjective 'this very pure‑minded / virtuous one.' The verb follows the ordinal, with the predicate phrase identifying who the fourth is.
segments.s475-g54: سر هوشمندان is a genitive/possessive construction 'the heads of the wise' (older Persian often places the possessed before the verb without an ezāfe). برآرد is a 3rd-person singular present/subjunctive meaning 'brings up / raises' (here 'will lift'). ز جای is a poetic prepositional phrase 'from their place / seats' with ز as 'from.' The object precedes the verb (typical in older Persian poetry) and the verb expresses action on that object.
segments.s475-g55: چنان is an adverb 'thus/so' setting the scene. چارسو is a compound noun 'the four quarters / four directions.' از پی is a prepositional phrase 'in pursuit of / following' and پاس را is the definite object 'the sentry/guard' marked by را; the object is fronted before the verb (a common poetic inversion).
segments.s475-g56: کشیدند is the simple past 3rd-person plural 'they pulled/dragged.' زانگونه 'in that way' modifies the verb. کرپاس را is the definite object 'the coarse linen / rag' with را. The verb placed here serves both the fronted object (پاس را) and the following object (کرپاس را), a linkage typical in poetic sentences where objects may be separated from the verb.
segments.s475-g121: که here introduces a subordinate/explanatory clause ('that'). دانش 'knowledge' is the clause's subject. نباشد is the negative present/subjunctive form of بودن used in narrative sense = 'is/was not (to be)'; به نزدیک اوی = 'near him' (به + نزدیک = 'at/near'; اوی is the old enclitic form of او meaning 'him'). The whole segment reads literally 'that knowledge was not near him.'
segments.s475-g122: پر از غم is an idiomatic adjective phrase 'full of sorrow' (پر = 'full (of)', از = 'of'), بود is the past copula 'was', and جان تاریک اوی = 'his soul/spirit was dark' (جان = 'soul/life', تاریک = 'dark/gloomy', اوی = possessive/enclitic 'his'). Word order places the predicative phrase 'پر از غم' before بود, producing 'his soul was full of sorrow, (his) soul dark.'
segments.s475-g123: همی is an adverb of habitual or continuous action ('always/continually'), هر زمان 'every time/season', نو 'new', کند is the present/habitual 3rd‑person singular of کردن meaning 'makes/raises', and لشکری is the object 'an army / troops'. Together: 'he repeatedly (at every time) made/raised a new army.'
segments.s475-g124: که starts a relative clause modifying 'لشکری'. سازند is 3rd‑person plural (subjunctive/optative or habitual) of ساختن/ساختن 'they make/compose'. ز + و here represent the poetic contraction 'زو' = از او ('from it / from him'); نامدار 'renowned', افسری is adjectival/noun meaning related to 'commander/officer' (i.e. 'a commander/one who wears the helmet'). The clause therefore says 'from it/from that army they make a famed commander' (i.e. 'from which they fashion a renowned leader').
segments.s475-g125: سرانجام 'at last/finally' sets the temporal frame. لشکر نماند is a negative past narrative: لشکر 'the army' + نماند (past of ماندن) 'did not remain/was not left'. نه شاه 'nor (the) king' — نه functions like 'nor' linking the two negated items. The clause means 'in the end neither army remained nor the king.'
segments.s475-g126: بیاید is an optative/present form often used in epic narrative to mean 'came/appeared' in this context. نو آیین 'a new custom/ceremony' is the subject, یکی پیش‌گاه 'before a certain court/a certain presence' indicates the locus. Together: 'a new custom appeared in/at a certain court.'
segments.s475-g127: کنون 'now' + این زمان 'this time' (demonstrative + noun) and روز اسکندرست is a nominal predicate 'the day belongs to Alexander' (اسکندرست = اسکندر + است, 'is Alexander's' or 'is Alexander'). The clause asserts the present era/day is that of Alexander.
segments.s475-g128: که introduces an explanatory clause. بر = 'on/upon', تارک here denotes the crown/upper part of the head (literary 'brow' or 'crown'), مهتران = 'the chiefs/seniors' (plural of مهتر), and افسرست is اسْت predicated to افسر (افسر + است) meaning 'a helmet/symbol of command is upon' — literally 'a helmet sits on the brow of the chiefs.' The clause explains why it is 'Alexander's day' (his helmet sits over the leaders).
segments.s475-g9: ترا is the archaic dative/object form 'to/for you' (ت + را). چار چیزست is an existential statement: چیزست = چیز است (‘there is a thing’), here with the numeral چار ‘four’ before it. کاندر جهان is a locative prepositional phrase ‘in the world’ (کاندر = 'in/within'), placed after the existential clause in classical word order.
segments.s475-g10: کسی آن ندید is a classical negative existential pattern: 'کسی' here functions as 'no one' (negative polarity) and ندید is the past-tense verb 'saw' (so together 'no one has seen that'). از introduces the source/group 'from/among', and کهان و مهان names the groups ('کهان' = the ancients/old ones, 'مهان' = the great/important people in context). Word order is subject (implicit) + object + verb in a terse, poetic arrangement.
segments.s475-g11: یکی 'one/a certain' begins the itemizing. چون = 'like/as', so 'یکی چون ...' = 'one like ...'. بهشت برین is a compound adjective: بر + ین form the adjective 'برین' (supreme/highest) modifying بهشت ('paradise'). دخترت is 'your daughter' (دختر + enclitic -ت = 'your'), placed after the descriptive phrase as in poetic order.
segments.s475-g12: This is a relative/attributive clause qualifying 'دخترت'. کزو = 'from whom / of whom' (ک + زو), introducing possession/source. تابد is the classical present/simple verbal form 'shines' (تافت→تابد) meaning 'shines upon'. اندر زمین 'upon the earth' is the locative, and افسرت (افسر + -ت) is 'your افسر' — here 'افسر' functions as helmet/diadem (crown). Together: 'from whom your crown shines on earth.'
segments.s475-g13: دگر = 'another' introduces the next item. فیلسوفی here names the figure (classically 'a philosopher'—the supplied sense options reflect the root but the line functions as a noun). که داری نهان is a relative clause: که = 'whom/that', داری = present 'you have/hold', and نهان = 'hidden' (adjective/adverb), so 'a philosopher whom you keep hidden.' The relative clause follows the head noun in classical order.
segments.s475-g14: This clause states the philosopher's action. بگوید is the classical 3rd-person present/future-form of گفتن ('he tells / will tell'); in poetry the prefix ب- often appears. همه = 'all/everything' is the object; با تو 'to you' (literally 'with you' but here 'to you'); راز جهان = 'the secrets of the world' (راز modified by جهان). Word order is verb – object – indirect object phrase typical of classical Persian poetry.
segments.s475-g15: سه دیگر = 'three others' (numeral + 'other') sets up the plural items. پزشکی in classical usage can denote the medical art or a physician by metonymy; here it denotes physicians. که هست ارجمند is a relative clause: که = 'who', هست = copula 'are', ارجمند = 'esteemed/precious', so 'three other physicians who are esteemed.'
segments.s475-g16: به دانندگی is an instrumental/adverbial phrase 'by/through knowledge' (به = 'by/through', دانندگی = 'knowledge/skill'). نام کرده بلند is a small result clause: نام (reputation), کرده (past participle/causative 'have made' or 'made'), بلند 'high/lofty' — literally 'have made their name lofty'. The verbal adjective کرده with بلند yields the perfect/resultive meaning common in classical Persian.
segments.s475-g33: ازین پس بیاید یکی پادشا is a compact temporal + verb + noun clause: ازین پس 'from now on' (از = from; این written as ین), بیاید is a subjunctive/optative or narrative-present meaning 'will come/comes', and یکی پادشا 'a certain/one king' is the subject introduced after the verb for a poetic word order.
segments.s475-g34: چنان سست و بی‌سود و ناپارسا is a predicate phrase describing that king; چنان 'such/so' modifies the following adjectives, which are coordinated by و 'and'. These are attributive adjectives (sest, bi-sud, na-pārsā) listing his qualities.
segments.s475-g35: به دل سفله باشد به تن ناتوان strings together two predications using parallel prepositional phrases: به + noun = 'in/at (the) [noun]'. سفله modifies دل ('base/vile of heart'). باشد is the copular verb (subjunctive/narrative 'is/will be') linking the qualities to the implied subject (the king).
segments.s475-g36: به آز اندرون نیز تیره‌روان continues the parallel description: به آز اندرون 'inwardly/from within by greed' (به + noun phrase), نیز 'also', and تیره‌روان is a compound adjective 'dark‑souled/ill‑fated'. Word order places the cause/ground (آز اندرون) before the resulting state.
segments.s475-g37: کجا زیردستانش باشند شاد uses کجا 'where/how (could)' rhetorically to introduce a question; زیردستانش 'his subjects' is the topic, باشند is the verb 'be' (subjunctive/optative/narrative form) and شاد 'happy' is the predicate. Poetic word order places the interrogative first.
segments.s475-g38: پر از غم دل شاه و لب پر ز باد is two coordinated nominal phrases explaining why they cannot be happy: 'پر از غم دل شاه' = 'the king's heart is full of grief' (پر از = full of; غم دل = grief of the heart), and 'لب پر ز باد' = 'his lips are full of wind' (ز/از = from/of in poetic register), the latter metaphor meaning empty or windy speech.
segments.s475-g39: دگر آنک دیدی ز کرپاس نغز begins with دگر 'again/furthermore', then آنک 'that/which' introduces a relative/content clause; دیدی is past 'you saw'. ز کرپاس 'from (a) kerpas/coarse cloth' modifies what was seen; نغز is an adjective meaning 'pithy/witty', here describing the speech or item associated with the rag.
segments.s475-g40: گرفته ورا چار پاکیزه مغز is a short clause meaning 'he has taken from him/fro m it four clean brains': گرفته is the past participle/verb 'taken/seized', ورا is the enclitic 'from him/it' (to/for him), چار here is 'four' (count), پاکیزه 'pure/clean' modifies مغز 'brains' (metaphorical for wits). Word order places verb before object in typical poetic compression.
segments.s475-g129: چو here is the temporal subordinating conjunction 'when' introducing the subordinate clause. آید is 3rd-person present ('comes'); بدو is the dative pronoun 'to him' marking the recipient. The imperative verb ده ('give') is understood after the clause and is addressed to تو ('you', emphatic subject); این چار چیز is the direct object 'these four things' (چار = 'four'). Word order is classical: subordinate clause (چو آید بدو) followed by the imperative instruction with subject after the verb.
segments.s475-g130: برآنم is a fixed first‑person present statement 'I am resolved/intend'. که introduces a content clause explaining that resolution. چیزی is the indefinite object 'anything/something'; نخواهد is 3rd‑person negative present/future of خواستن 'will not want' (here 'will not desire'). به نیز is a postpositional phrase 'besides / beyond that' (نیز = 'also/besides'), limiting the object. Together: 'I am resolved that he will not want anything besides (those things).'
segments.s475-g131: چو here functions as a conditional 'if' introducing the condition. خشنود (pleased/content) is a predicate adjective used with داشتن; داری is 2nd‑person present of داشتن 'you keep/have', and ورا is the object pronoun 'him' (to him). بگذرد is 3rd‑person present subjunctive/indicative of گذشتن; in this context it means 'he will let it pass / forgive it' (i.e. not make trouble). The clause reads 'If you keep him content, he will let it pass.'
segments.s475-g132: که introduces the reason/explanation 'for/because'. دانش پژوَه‌ست is a compact predicate: دانش 'learning' + پژوهست (is‑seeking) = 'he is a seeker of knowledge' (پژوهست = 'is seeking'). و دارد خرد is a coordinate clause 'and he possesses wisdom' (دارد = 'has', خرد = 'wisdom'). The whole segment gives the motive: 'for he seeks learning and has wisdom.'
segments.s475-g133: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' (here introducing the person involved) and مهران is the proper name; the construction 'ز X چو فعل' commonly means 'when X [did the verb]'. چو is the temporal 'when' and بشنید is past 3rd‑person 'he heard'. کید این سخن is the object phrase 'this plot/contrivance of the speech' (کید = 'plot', این سخن = 'this talk'). Word order places the source (ز مهران) before the verb as an emphatic/poetic arrangement.
segments.s475-g134: برو here is a short narrative particle (literally 'go') used idiomatically as 'thereupon' or 'then' in classical Persian storytelling. تازه شد is 'became fresh/new' (شد = 'became'), and روزگار کهن means 'the ancient/old time' or 'old days'. Together: 'thereupon the old days were renewed' (i.e. former conditions returned).
segments.s475-g135: بیامد is past 'he came'. سر و چشم او is a coordinated object 'his head and eyes' (common formulaic objects for respectful kissing). بوس is the noun 'a kiss' used with داد 'gave' (past 'gave') to form the idiom 'gave a kiss' = 'kissed him'. The clause sequence is narrative: he came, and (then) kissed his head and eyes.
segments.s475-g136: دلارام و پیروز is a coordinated subject ('Dolaram and Pirooz'). برگشت is a verbal noun/verb 'returned' used here as the past action, and شاد is a predicative adjective placed after the verb meaning 'happy' or 'joyful'. So: 'Dolaram and Pirooz returned happy.' (پیروز here functions as a proper name/epithet in the subject position.)

sentences.s475-s49: After this there will come a time when the dervish becomes so weak and despised.
sentences.s475-s50: If the clouds become full of water in spring, the sun hides itself from the dervish.
sentences.s475-s51: Nor does even his own rain fall on him; the dervish man's heart from it has become a beard.
sentences.s475-s52: The rich man will bestow this upon one, and (also) upon another who is oily and sweet‑tongued.
sentences.s475-s69: When the king returned from the vicinity of the sage, the sages went with him on the road.
sentences.s475-s33: If one acts like a fish, he will end up in the sea; even if through his bad conduct he should be raised to the Pleiades.
sentences.s475-s34: He continually summons the thirsty to the water; no one can compose for him an answer from learning.
sentences.s475-s35: They flee from that learned man; they open their lips among bad companions.
sentences.s475-s36: As for the fifth (one) that you saw, there is a town; within it he has constructed a marvel.
sentences.s475-s21: The fine kerpas did not tear from being pulled, nor did he who pulled it become weary.
sentences.s475-s22: After this there will come a renowned one — a spearman from among the plain horsemen.
sentences.s475-s23: A clean, good‑natured man — to him God's religion will be established in every direction.
sentences.s475-s24: There will be an old peasant fire‑worshipper who will take in hand to overturn custom.
sentences.s475-s41: Those who praise become ignorant men; praying, they come before God.
sentences.s475-s42: One always knows the person who tells a lie; therefore from that worship he does not gain radiance.
sentences.s475-s43: Sixth: that which you saw on a two‑headed steed — its food did not pass straight across.
sentences.s475-s44: A time will come when people will be pleased with a thing and likewise will not find satiety.
sentences.s475-s37: Full of give-and-take, buying and selling — you would say at that time their eyes were sewn shut.
sentences.s475-s38: From blindness one did not see the other; each would not look at the other.
sentences.s475-s39: A time will come from which things will be such that the wise will become a worshipper (or dependent) of the ignorant.
sentences.s475-s40: To them a man of learning was despised; the tree of their wisdom does not come to fruit.
sentences.s475-s29: Then, for the sake of guard, four slayers — God‑knowing in religion — came to the shroud.
sentences.s475-s30: Ever he draws this from that and that from this; at that time enemies arise on account of religion.
sentences.s475-s31: Another — a thirsty one who turned away from sweet water — fleeing; and the fish is for it a sieve.
sentences.s475-s32: A time will come when a pure man will be held in contempt, as he drinks the water of knowledge.
sentences.s475-s13: If we accept/apply Farrokh's counsel about your matter, we will likewise answer that very dream.
sentences.s475-s14: You saw a certain house and a narrow hole, from which an elephant immediately came out.
sentences.s475-s15: You, like a knower of the world, (regarded) that house; that very royal elephant was ungrateful.
sentences.s475-s16: For he is a tyrant and a perverse speaker; apart from the name/title of kingship there is no originality in him.
sentences.s475-s9: Fourth: a goblet of kandro‑rizi water — it is diminished by neither fire nor by the sun.
sentences.s475-s10: It will not lose much of its water through eating; with these things, set the water on the surface.
sentences.s475-s11: When it comes, be by this (follow this) and a miskal of jang; if you wish that here there be no delay.
sentences.s475-s12: You will not be sufficient against his army — not by stratagem, nor by treasure, nor by his commander.
sentences.s475-s45: Neither will a dervish obtain any share from it, nor scholarship, nor fame.
sentences.s475-s46: They will have no recourse except to themselves; there will be no one to help them.
sentences.s475-s47: On the seventh, when you saw three vessels full of water, one of them remained empty — a bad thing to my hurt.
sentences.s475-s48: Two (of them) were full of perennial water throughout; the middle one was dry and childless — a bad thing.
sentences.s475-s53: It happens that the poor man, dry-lipped, spends the day and passes it into night.
sentences.s475-s54: Another — that cow, so well-bodied, milk sprang from her thin calf.
sentences.s475-s55: When Saturn comes into the sign of the Scales, the world will be under the force of the arm.
sentences.s475-s56: Work becomes sick and the poor man weak; from that he will long for, indeed, a sound body.
sentences.s475-s57: He never opens the lid of his treasure, nor does he use it to relieve the suffering of his body.
sentences.s475-s58: Then you saw another spring of clear water, and about it were waters like musk.
sentences.s475-s59: Nor did you take away a single bright drop from it, nor did you seize those waters in haste.
sentences.s475-s60: After this, there came a certain time when there was sovereignty in the world.
sentences.s475-s1: When Mehrān heard these words of a plot, he said to him, "Do not let your heart be troubled by this dream."
sentences.s475-s2: Neither will your lofty name be diminished, nor will harm come to this kingship.
sentences.s475-s3: Alexander will bring a great army — chosen leaders from Rome and from Iran.
sentences.s475-s4: If you wish your good name to remain, make wisdom your ally; do not seek his battle.
sentences.s475-s25: Another is the religion of Moses — which you call the Jews — who say that nothing but that ought to be praised.
sentences.s475-s26: Another is the Greek faith — that pious one who brought justice into the king's heart.
sentences.s475-s27: Fourth will come this very pure-minded man, who will lift the heads of the wise from their seats.
sentences.s475-s28: Thus, from all four directions, in pursuit of the guard they dragged him; in that manner they drew the coarse linen.
sentences.s475-s61: That no learning was near him; his soul was full of sorrow and his spirit was dark.
sentences.s475-s62: He repeatedly raised a new army at every turn, from which they made a renowned commander.
sentences.s475-s63: At last no army remained and no king (either); a new custom came before a certain court.
sentences.s475-s64: Now this time is Alexander's day: his helmet sits upon the brows of the chiefs.
sentences.s475-s5: There are four things for you in the world that no one among the ancients and great ones has seen.
sentences.s475-s6: One is a daughter like the highest paradise, from whom your helmet (crown) shines upon the earth.
sentences.s475-s7: Another is a philosopher whom you keep hidden; he will tell you all the secrets of the world.
sentences.s475-s8: Three others are physicians who are esteemed; by knowledge they have made their name high.
sentences.s475-s17: From now on there will come a king who is so weak, useless, and impious.
sentences.s475-s18: He will be base at heart, weak in body, and inwardly dark‑souled from greed.
sentences.s475-s19: How could his subjects be joyful, when the king's heart is full of sorrow and his lips full of wind?
sentences.s475-s20: Again — what you saw: from a coarse rag he had taken four clean brains.
sentences.s475-s65: When they come to him, give him these four things; I am resolved that he will not want anything more.
sentences.s475-s66: If you keep him content, he will let it pass, for he is a seeker of knowledge and possesses wisdom.
sentences.s475-s67: When Mehran heard of this plot, the old days were renewed.
sentences.s475-s68: He came and kissed his head and eyes; Dolaram and Pirooz returned happily.
